findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `!.rebar/metrics-snapshots.ndjson` to `.gitignore` (mirroring the pattern for `criteria_routing.json` at line 26) and include this `.gitignore` change in the Files section — the blanket `.rebar/*` rule currently means the snapshot file would be git-ignored and the trend series would silently never accumulate.
evidence: Plan: '.rebar/ is already tracked in git (e.g. .rebar/criteria_routing.json), so snapshots persist and sync'
.gitignore line 20: `.rebar/*` (blanket ignore of ALL .rebar/ contents)
.gitignore lines 21,26,27,28,30,31,33: only specific surgical `!.rebar/<file>` negations un-ignore tracked files
.gitignore line 26: `!.rebar/criteria_routing.json` — criteria_routing.json is tracked via this explicit negation, NOT because .rebar/ is generally tracked
No `!.rebar/metrics-snapshots.ndjson` negation exists anywhere in .gitignore
AC3 test 'asserting the file is not matched by the repo's .gitignore' would itself fail without adding the negation rule
findings.finding: The plan asserts '.rebar/ is already tracked in git' (citing `.rebar/criteria_routing.json` as evidence), but the actual `.gitignore` has a blanket `.rebar/*` rule (line 20) that ignores ALL `.rebar/` contents — each tracked file requires a surgical `!.rebar/<file>` negation. There is no `!.rebar/metrics-snapshots.ndjson` negation anywhere, so the new snapshot file would be git-ignored and the trend series would silently never accumulate — the exact failure mode the plan warns about for `reports/`.
findings.location: Files section / Approach section — assertion '`.rebar/` is already tracked in git (e.g. `.rebar/criteria_routing.json`)'; AC3 — 'asserting the file is not matched by the repo's `.gitignore`'
scenarios: A developer creates `.rebar/metrics-snapshots.ndjson` per the plan; git status shows it as untracked/ignored; it is never committed; the trend series never accumulates.
The AC3 gitignore-check test passes locally only if the implementer happens to add the negation rule, but the plan does not specify that `.gitignore` must be updated — leaving it as an invisible gap.
CI appends snapshots each run but they are never committed (ignored), so the 'going-forward' series is permanently empty.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Files section: '`.gitignore` — add `!.rebar/metrics-snapshots.ndjson` negation (mirrors `!.rebar/criteria_routing.json` at line 26; required because `.rebar/*` at line 20 blanket-ignores all `.rebar/` contents).' Also update AC3 to explicitly verify the negation rule is present.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `.gitignore` to the Files section and include a `!.rebar/metrics-snapshots.ndjson` surgical un-ignore line (parallel to `!.rebar/criteria_routing.json`), otherwise the snapshot file is gitignored and never committed.
evidence: .gitignore line 20: `.rebar/*`
Un-ignore exceptions cover only: trusted_environments.yaml, criteria_routing.json, plan_review_moves.json, prompts/, evals/ — not metrics-snapshots.ndjson
Plan states '.rebar/ is already tracked in git' but that is only true for surgically excepted files
AC3 says test should assert 'the file is not matched by the repo's .gitignore' — this test would fail without the gitignore exception being added
No entry for `.gitignore` in the plan's Files section

The plan never lists `.gitignore` as a file to edit, so `!.rebar/metrics-snapshots.ndjson` is never added — the new snapshot file would be silently ignored by git, directly defeating the plan's stated rationale for storing it under `.rebar/`.
findings.location: Files section / AC3 / .gitignore line 20
scenarios: Developer runs CI; write_snapshot appends to .rebar/metrics-snapshots.ndjson; git status shows the file as untracked/ignored; it is never committed; the trend series never accumulates — silently, exactly as the plan warns about /reports/.

evidence: .gitignore line 20: `.rebar/*` — blanket ignore of all .rebar/ contents

.gitignore lines 21–33: only `!.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml`, `!.rebar/criteria_routing.json`, `!.rebar/plan_review_moves.json`, `!.rebar/prompts/`, `!.rebar/evals/` are negated — no negation for metrics-snapshots.ndjson

Update AC3 to assert that this negation is present and that `git check-ignore -v .rebar/metrics-snapshots.ndjson` returns no match.

evidence: Plan quote: '.rebar/ is already tracked in git (e.g. .rebar/criteria_routing.json), so snapshots persist and sync.'
.gitignore line 20: `.rebar/*` (blanket wildcard ignore of all .rebar/ contents)

.gitignore lines 21-33: only specific surgical negations exist — `!.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml`, `!.rebar/criteria_routing.json`, `!.rebar/plan_review_moves.json`, `!.rebar/prompts/`, `!.rebar/evals/` — `metrics-snapshots.ndjson` is absent
AC3 would assert 'the file is not matched by the repo's .gitignore' — this assertion would FAIL as written because `.rebar/*` matches the new file
Corroborating prior plan-review cache finding at `.rebar/cache/plan-review/3275-b164-2e9b-4e66/af28d50388d9fc25f75d.json` and `81b532b772c2efb1d2f0.json` flagging the same gap
findings.finding: The plan asserts '.rebar/ is already tracked in git (e.g. .rebar/criteria_routing.json), so snapshots persist and sync' — but the actual .gitignore has a blanket `.rebar/*` wildcard on line 20 that ignores ALL .rebar/ contents. Only a small set of surgically named exceptions exist (`!.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml`, `!.rebar/criteria_routing.json`, `!.rebar/plan_review_moves.json`, etc.). `.rebar/metrics-snapshots.ndjson` has no such negation, so it would be gitignored and the trend series would silently never accumulate — the exact failure mode the plan claims to avoid by not using `reports/`.
findings.location: Files section ('`.rebar/metrics-snapshots.ndjson` — new committed artifact') and Approach section ('It lives under the tracked `.rebar/` store so it is committed and synced'); also AC3 ('verified by the test … asserting the file is not matched by the repo's .gitignore')
scenarios: A developer implements the plan as written, runs CI, and `write_snapshot` appends to `.rebar/metrics-snapshots.ndjson`. The file is never committed (gitignored by `.rebar/*`), so the trend series is permanently empty — the going-forward series never accrues.

AC3's gitignore assertion passes locally only if the implementor manually adds the `!` negation — but because the plan never specifies this `.gitignore` change as a required deliverable, it will be omitted.

Also update AC3 to assert the negation line is present: `assert '!.rebar/metrics-snapshots.ndjson' in Path('.gitignore').read_text()`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `!.rebar/metrics-snapshots.ndjson` to `.gitignore` (after the `.rebar/*` blanket rule, alongside the other surgical negations) so the snapshot file is git-tracked; the plan must specify this change in the Files section, or the snapshot series will be silently ignored under the existing `.rebar/*` glob.
evidence: `.gitignore` line 20: `.rebar/*` — blanket glob ignores all `.rebar/` contents

`.gitignore` lines 21–33: only 7 `!`-negation exceptions exist: `!.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml`, `!.rebar/criteria_routing.json`, `!.rebar/plan_review_moves.json`, `!.rebar/prompts/`, `!.rebar/prompts/plan-review-project-portability.md`, `!.rebar/evals/`, `!.rebar/evals/plan-review-project-portability.eval.yaml`
`!.rebar/metrics-snapshots.ndjson` is absent from ALL negation entries — CONTRADICTED evidence class
Plan claims: '.rebar/ is already tracked in git (e.g. `.rebar/criteria_routing.json`)' — that file is tracked via its own surgical negation, not because `.rebar/` as a whole is tracked
Plan's cited contrast ('unlike the gitignored `reports/`') is logically undermined: `.rebar/metrics-snapshots.ndjson` would also be gitignored without a matching negation entry
findings.finding: The plan asserts `.rebar/` is 'already tracked in git' and that placing `.rebar/metrics-snapshots.ndjson` there will make the snapshot series persist and sync — but the actual `.gitignore` has a blanket `.rebar/*` rule (line 20) that ignores ALL `.rebar/` contents, with only 7 specific files/dirs surgically un-ignored via `!`-negations. `.rebar/metrics-snapshots.ndjson` is absent from every negation. The file would be silently git-ignored — the exact failure mode the plan claims to avoid. AC line 3's test asserting the file 'is not matched by the repo's `.gitignore`' would fail under the current configuration.
findings.location: Files section ('.rebar/metrics-snapshots.ndjson'), Approach section ('lives under the tracked `.rebar/` store'), AC line 3 ('asserting the file is not matched by the repo's `.gitignore`')
scenarios: CI runs `write_snapshot()` and the file is created at `.rebar/metrics-snapshots.ndjson`, but `git status` shows it as untracked/ignored — no `git add` or commit can include it, and the trend series silently never accumulates.

AC line 3's unit test calls `git check-ignore .rebar/metrics-snapshots.ndjson` (or equivalent) and receives a positive match from the `.rebar/*` rule, causing the assertion 'file is not matched by the repo's `.gitignore`' to fail.
A developer adds the file, commits, and pushes — git silently skips it due to the blanket ignore rule, with no warning.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Files section, add `.gitignore` as a file to modify, with the entry `!.rebar/metrics-snapshots.ndjson` inserted after line 20's `.rebar/*` rule (following the same surgical-negation pattern used for `criteria_routing.json` and `trusted_environments.yaml`). The AC line 3 test wording already implies this check — ensuring it passes requires the negation to exist first.

evidence: 2 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace AC3's vague 'asserting the file is not matched by the repo's .gitignore' with a concrete proving command (e.g., `git check-ignore -v .rebar/metrics-snapshots.ndjson` exits non-zero, meaning not ignored) run from the repo root, and note whether this is a CI shell check or a pytest subprocess call.
evidence: AC3 says 'asserting the file is not matched by the repo's .gitignore' but gives no concrete command (e.g., `git check-ignore -v .rebar/metrics-snapshots.ndjson`)
Unit tests typically write to `tmp_path` fixtures, not the repo root; a test writing to `tmp_path/.rebar/` would not be in the repo's gitignore scope
Verify-command defect taxonomy item 7 (missing prerequisite-state ordering): the file must exist in the repo worktree for `git check-ignore` to be meaningful
findings.finding: AC3's proving mechanism — 'asserting the file is not matched by the repo's .gitignore' — has no concrete executable command specified, and the assertion may be unexecutable as a standard unit test: it requires a git worktree context (to call `git check-ignore`) and the file path must exist or be explicitly passed; a unit test running under `pytest` in a temp directory would fail this check even if the path is correct.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC3: 'verified by the test writing under `.rebar/` and asserting the file is not matched by the repo's `.gitignore`'
scenarios: The unit test writes the snapshot to a `tmp_path` directory and calls `git check-ignore` on a path outside the repo, returning exit code 128 (not a repo), causing the test to error rather than assert

The AC is satisfied by a trivially-passing assertion that doesn't actually verify gitignore behavior (e.g., asserting the string `.rebar/` is not in `.gitignore` file contents via a grep, which could false-positive)
findings.suggested_fix: Change AC3 to: 'A shell check `git check-ignore -v .rebar/metrics-snapshots.ndjson` exits with code 1 (not ignored) when run from the repo root — assert this in CI via `pytest` subprocess or a Makefile target, not via string-parsing `.gitignore`.`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the types of `since` and `until` in the plan (e.g., 'ISO 8601 datetime strings; `None` means unbounded') so the signature and test fixture are unambiguous.
evidence: Plan states `read_snapshots(since,until) yields in-range records` without specifying parameter types
AC1 uses `read_snapshots(since,until)` in the proving assertion without stating what `since`/`until` are
findings.finding: The `since` and `until` parameter types accepted by `read_snapshots(since, until)` are not specified — an implementor does not know whether to accept ISO 8601 strings, `datetime` objects, Unix timestamps, or date strings, and the AC test assertion `assert rec in read_snapshots(since, until)` relies on these types being consistent.
findings.location: Files section: `read_snapshots(since,until)` signature; AC1: `assert rec in read_snapshots(since,until)`
scenarios: An agent implements `since`/`until` as `datetime` objects but the test passes strings, causing a runtime TypeError that isn't caught by the AC
An agent uses Unix timestamps while the stored records use ISO 8601 strings, silently returning zero records
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Files section: '`read_snapshots(since: str | None, until: str | None)` — ISO 8601 datetime strings (e.g. `"2024-01-01T00:00:00Z"`); `None` means unbounded in that direction.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a minimal snapshot schema to the plan (e.g., `{"coverage_pct": float, "complexity": {...}, "clone_count": int}`) so the unit test fixture and the writer contract are unambiguous.
evidence: Plan says 'Coverage/complexity/clone are computed at a point in time' but does not name the exact keys or value types for the dict argument
The unit test must construct a valid snapshot dict but has no schema to follow
findings.finding: The snapshot dict schema (which keys and value types `write_snapshot(dict)` expects) is never formally specified; an implementor writing the unit test must guess what fields constitute a valid snapshot record.
findings.location: Files section: `write_snapshot(dict)` description; Tests section
scenarios: Implementor uses key `coverage_pct` but the command consumer expects `coverage`; the round-trip test passes but the integration silently fails
Complexity distribution is a dict vs a float — the test fixture is arbitrary and may not match real usage
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Approach or Files section: 'Each snapshot record has at minimum the keys `coverage_pct` (float), `complexity_distribution` (dict), `clone_count` (int), and `timestamp` (ISO 8601 string added by `write_snapshot`).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define a RETIRE/pruning policy for `metrics-snapshots.ndjson` (e.g., keep last N records, a rolling time window, or a documented manual truncation procedure) so the file does not grow without bound.
evidence: 'append-only' — no size cap, rotation, pruning window, or archive step mentioned anywhere in the plan
The file is committed to git, so every append is a new git blob; git does not delta-compress growing text files as efficiently as it does stable ones
No RETIRE checklist item exists in the acceptance criteria
findings.finding: The append-only NDJSON file has no retirement or pruning path, so it grows by one record per CI run indefinitely. Both the working-tree file and git history accumulate without bound. For a project with daily CI, years of records become a non-trivial git blob.
findings.location: Approach section — 'append-only NDJSON snapshot'
scenarios: A project running CI 5× per day accumulates ~1825 records/year; after 3 years the file and its git history become a meaningful overhead with no documented remediation path

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a deduplication mechanism to `write_snapshot` (e.g., a mandatory `run_id` field) and specify that `read_snapshots` (or a write guard) deduplicates on that key so retried jobs do not corrupt the trend series.
evidence: 'write_snapshot(dict) appends a timestamped record' — no deduplication key, run-id field, or idempotency guard mentioned
CI retry (e.g. GitHub Actions 're-run failed jobs') re-executes the full job, calling write_snapshot again
The acceptance criteria test only round-trip correctness; no test covers duplicate suppression
findings.finding: `write_snapshot` is not idempotent: a retried or re-run CI job appends a second record for the same run, producing duplicate timestamps in the series. Consumers (`read_snapshots`) return both, silently inflating or distorting trend metrics.
findings.location: Approach section — 'append-only NDJSON snapshot (not a new event type)'
scenarios: A flaky CI run is retried → write_snapshot appends a second record with an identical or near-identical timestamp → read_snapshots yields two records for the same run → trend computation double-counts the data point
findings.suggested_fix: Include a `run_id` field (e.g. CI_RUN_ID env var or a content hash) in every snapshot record; have write_snapshot skip the append if a record with that run_id already exists in the tail of the file.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a unit test asserting that `read_snapshots` on a non-existent (or empty) NDJSON file yields zero records without raising, covering the documented 'unavailable' first-run contract.
evidence: Plan states: 'until a snapshot exists a metric reports unavailable'
AC1 only tests round-trip (file exists with data) and out-of-range exclusion
No AC or test entry covers read_snapshots on a non-existent or zero-record file
This is the first-run state for every new consumer of the API
findings.finding: The plan documents that 'until a snapshot exists a metric reports unavailable', establishing a defined behavioral contract for the missing-file / empty-file case, but no unit test covers `read_snapshots` when the NDJSON file does not yet exist or is empty — leaving this documented contract unverified.
findings.location: Tests section and AC1; plan prose 'until a snapshot exists a metric reports unavailable'
scenarios: First CI run before any snapshot is written: command calls read_snapshots on a nonexistent file — if it raises FileNotFoundError instead of yielding empty, the command breaks
Empty file after a truncation or reset: reader must not raise
findings.suggested_fix: In `tests/unit/metrics/test_snapshot.py`, add a test that calls `read_snapshots(since, until)` pointing at a path that does not exist (or a tmp empty file) and asserts `list(read_snapshots(...)) == []` — no exception raised. Also update AC1 or add AC4 to make this an acceptance criterion.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether `write_snapshot`'s flock-guarded append and `read_snapshots`'s malformed-line-skip can reuse or be co-located with the identical patterns in `alert_store.append()` / `import_ndjson._iter_records()`, or document why a separate implementation in `metrics/snapshot.py` is warranted.
evidence: `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/alert_store.py:40-59` implements `append(record, repo_root)`: `open(path, 'a')` + `fcntl.LOCK_EX` + `json.dumps(record) + '\n'` + `f.flush()` — identical to what `write_snapshot` will do.

`src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/alert_store.py:62-81` implements `is_deduped()` which iterates NDJSON lines and `except`-skips unparseable ones — the same read-and-skip pattern `read_snapshots` will implement.
`src/rebar/_io/import_ndjson.py:54-74` implements `_iter_records()` with explicit `json.JSONDecodeError` catch-and-warn-and-skip for malformed lines.
Plan does not reference or justify non-reuse of these existing implementations.
findings.finding: `write_snapshot` (flock-guarded append of a JSON line) and the malformed-line-skip in `read_snapshots` re-implement patterns already present in `alert_store.append()` and `import_ndjson._iter_records()` respectively, without citing why those cannot be reused or factored.
findings.location: Files section — `src/rebar/metrics/snapshot.py`; Approach section
scenarios: Two maintenance sites exist for the same locking/skipping logic; a future fix to the append lock pattern must be applied in two places.
If `alert_store.append` gains an improvement (e.g. better error handling), `snapshot.py` silently stays behind.
findings.suggested_fix: Either import and delegate to `alert_store.append` for the write path (if the subsystem boundary allows it), or extract a shared `rebar._store.ndjson_util.append_line` / `iter_records` helper that both `alert_store` and `snapshot` use — keeping the locking contract in one place.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define and enforce an idempotency guarantee for `write_snapshot` (e.g., include a unique run-id or content-hash key in each record, and specify that re-running the writer for the same run either skips the write or overwrites the existing record rather than appending a duplicate).
evidence: Plan says 'append-only' but specifies no deduplication key or idempotency guard in write_snapshot.
AC tests round-trip and range-filter correctness, and malformed-line skipping — but no AC covers duplicate-append prevention.
ABSENCE: no mention of a run-id, content-hash, or 'already written for this run' check before appending.
findings.finding: write_snapshot is not specified to be idempotent: if a CI job appends a snapshot record and then fails for an unrelated reason, a re-run of that job will append a second duplicate record for the same timestamp/run, causing the trend series to count the same point twice and skew all derived metrics.
findings.location: Files section — `src/rebar/metrics/snapshot.py`; Approach section; AC (no idempotency AC)
scenarios: CI job appends snapshot, then a downstream step fails and the job is retried — two identical timestamp records accumulate.
Developer runs the command locally multiple times on the same commit — snapshot file grows with duplicates, trend graphs show false spikes.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `run_id` field (e.g., `$CI_JOB_ID` or a hash of timestamp+metrics) to each snapshot record. In `write_snapshot`, scan the tail of the NDJSON file (or a separate index) for a matching `run_id` before appending; if found, skip or overwrite. Document this contract in the AC as: 'calling write_snapshot twice with the same run_id results in exactly one record in the file.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add either (a) a function/entry in Files that implements the 'unavailable' return when no snapshot exists, plus an AC that verifies it, or (b) remove the 'unavailable' claim from the Approach if it belongs to a different plan.
evidence: Approach: 'until a snapshot exists a metric reports `unavailable`'
Files section lists only write_snapshot and read_snapshots — no function for signaling unavailability
No AC tests or verifies the 'unavailable' state when no snapshot file exists
findings.finding: The Approach section describes a behavior — 'until a snapshot exists a metric reports `unavailable`' — that has no implementing function in the Files section and no covering acceptance criterion, making it an uncovered described deliverable.
findings.location: Approach section ('until a snapshot exists a metric reports `unavailable`') vs Files section and Acceptance Criteria
scenarios: An implementer reading the Approach will try to implement the 'unavailable' return, but there is no AC to verify it and no file/function specified to house the logic — the feature either gets silently dropped or ends up in an unspecified location.
A reviewer checking AC coverage will not find a criterion for the 'unavailable' state, so the behavior can regress undetected.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Files: 'read_snapshots returns an empty iterator (and callers treat an absent file as `unavailable`) when `.rebar/metrics-snapshots.ndjson` does not yet exist.' Add AC: 'When no snapshot file exists, read_snapshots returns an empty sequence and the caller can distinguish this from a zero-record file.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Capture the NDJSON-under-`.rebar/` architectural decision (rationale for committed file vs ephemeral `reports/`, append-only contract, consumer interface) in an ADR or AGENTS.md update listed in the Files section.
evidence: 'It lives under the tracked `.rebar/` store, NOT under `reports/`' — rationale exists only in the ticket
No mention of ADR, AGENTS.md, or design-doc update anywhere in the plan
ABSENCE: no documentation artifact is listed in the Files section
findings.finding: The novel architectural decision (committed NDJSON under `.rebar/` as the durable metrics store, chosen over ephemeral `reports/`) is explained only in the ticket body and will not be discoverable by future contributors once the ticket is closed. No ADR, AGENTS.md update, or design-doc entry is planned.
findings.location: ## Approach / ## Files sections
scenarios: A future contributor adds a new metrics store or moves the file, not knowing the gitignore constraint
A new team member wonders why snapshots are committed files rather than a lightweight DB or event log and cannot find the decision record
findings.suggested_fix: Add `.rebar/ADR-metrics-snapshot.md` (or an entry in `AGENTS.md`) to the Files list, documenting: why `.rebar/` not `reports/`, why NDJSON over alternatives, the append-only contract, and what consumers may depend on.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify write-path reliability for `write_snapshot`: define the error-handling strategy (e.g., log and surface failures rather than silently dropping), a locking or atomic-rename approach to prevent interleaved concurrent writes, and a deduplication/idempotency rule (e.g., skip if a record with the same run-id/timestamp already exists) — and add at least one unit test covering a simulated write failure or duplicate-write scenario.
evidence: 'write_snapshot(dict) appends a timestamped record' — no mention of locking, atomicity, or error handling on the write path
'Append-only' — acknowledged pattern but race/failure behavior unspecified
ABSENCE: no AC or test covers write failure, concurrent writes, or duplicate-run deduplication
CI environments commonly run parallel jobs that could append simultaneously
findings.finding: The `write_snapshot` append operation has no specified error handling, locking, or atomicity strategy: a failed append (disk full, permissions), a concurrent CI write race producing an interleaved/partial line, or a duplicate write (same CI run retried) will silently corrupt or inflate the snapshot series — directly undermining the plan's stated goal that 'their movement accrues'.
findings.location: ## Files: `src/rebar/metrics/snapshot.py` / ## Acceptance Criteria
scenarios: Two parallel CI jobs append at the same time, producing an interleaved or truncated NDJSON line (mitigated by skip-malformed on read, but the record is permanently lost)
A CI run is retried after a transient failure; the snapshot is written twice for the same point in time, inflating trend metrics
Disk full mid-append leaves a partial line; future read correctly skips it but the data point is silently dropped with no operator signal
findings.suggested_fix: Use a file lock (e.g., `fcntl.flock` or a `.lock` sidecar) around the append, wrap the write in a try/except that logs the error and re-raises (so CI fails loudly rather than silently), and document that callers should include a unique `run_id` field so downstream consumers can deduplicate.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify a concurrency-safe append mechanism for `write_snapshot` (e.g., `fcntl.flock` exclusive lock, write-then-atomic-rename, or a serialization gate) so parallel CI jobs cannot produce torn NDJSON lines.
evidence: 'write_snapshot(dict) appends a timestamped record' — no lock, fcntl, atomic rename, or exclusive-open specified
CI plans commonly run parallel jobs (e.g. coverage + complexity in separate matrix steps) that could call write_snapshot at the same time
The plan's only concurrency acknowledgment is the malformed-line skip, which is a reader-side workaround, not a writer-side safety guarantee
findings.finding: Concurrent appends from parallel CI jobs have no locking or atomic-write guarantee. Multiple jobs calling write_snapshot simultaneously can produce interleaved/torn NDJSON lines that the reader cannot parse as valid records. The plan's mitigation (skip malformed lines) treats the symptom but not the cause; data loss on concurrent writes is unaddressed.
findings.location: Approach section / AC line 3 (corrupt line skipped)
scenarios: CI matrix with two parallel jobs both call write_snapshot at the same moment → OS-level interleaved writes produce a single partially-written line → reader skips it → one snapshot is silently lost, breaking the trend series
findings.suggested_fix: Open the file with an exclusive flock before appending (fcntl.flock(fd, fcntl.LOCK_EX)) and release after the write, or write to a temp file and atomically rename it into place after merging.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add documentation or a setup check that instructs consumer projects to ensure `.rebar/metrics-snapshots.ndjson` is not gitignored in their own repo (e.g., a `git check-ignore` call at first write, or a note in the onboarding guide), and update the AC to clarify that the gitignore test is a proxy for consumer-side verification, not a complete guarantee.
evidence: 'verified by the test writing under `.rebar/` and asserting the file is not matched by the repo's `.gitignore`' — 'the repo' in a unit test context is the rebar harness repo, not the consumer project repo
The plan's entire rationale for `.rebar/` over `reports/` is gitignore safety: 'so the going-forward series actually persists'
Target projects (Ruby gems, Java Maven projects, .NET solutions) commonly gitignore dot-directories or all `*.ndjson`/`*.json` files at repo root
findings.finding: The AC gitignore check validates only the rebar project's own `.gitignore`, but the snapshot must be committed in the TARGET project's repository. A target project (Ruby, Java, .NET, etc.) may have `.gitignore` rules that exclude `.rebar/` or `*.ndjson`, causing the snapshot file to be silently ignored by git in that project's repo — exactly the failure mode the plan is designed to prevent.
findings.location: AC line 3 — 'asserting the file is not matched by the repo's `.gitignore`'
scenarios: A Next.js or Java target project has `.gitignore` containing `.rebar/` or `**/*.ndjson` → snapshots are written but never staged by git → `git status` shows them as ignored → trend series silently never accumulates, reproducing the exact failure the plan aims to prevent, but in the actual consumer environment rather than the test environment
findings.suggested_fix: In write_snapshot, after first creating the file, run `git check-ignore --quiet <path>` and emit a warning (not a hard failure) if the path is gitignored, so consumer-project users are alerted rather than silently losing their series.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate the `rebar metrics` command (or the ticket that will create it) as an explicit out-of-scope consumer in the Files/Approach section, or clarify that `read_snapshots` is designed for a future ticket and no current callers are updated by this change.
evidence: Approach: 'The command reads it for trend metrics, and until a snapshot exists a metric reports `unavailable`'
src/rebar/_commands/ contains no metrics.py or equivalent command file
Files section lists only snapshot.py, metrics-snapshots.ndjson, and test_snapshot.py — no command file
No ticket dependency on a metrics command ticket is mentioned
findings.finding: The plan's Approach section states 'The command reads it for trend metrics' referring to a `rebar metrics` command, but no such command exists in `src/rebar/_commands/` and the plan does not enumerate it as an out-of-scope dependency or future consumer. This is an unenumerated caller that would need to import `read_snapshots`.
findings.location: Approach section — 'The command reads it for trend metrics'
scenarios: The plan ships write/read primitives but the described consumer (the metrics command) is neither in scope nor enumerated as a dependent ticket, leaving `read_snapshots` with no current caller and the plan's stated use-case incomplete.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the concurrent-write safety strategy for `write_snapshot` — e.g. acquire an exclusive file lock (`fcntl.flock`) before appending, or adopt a per-run sidecar-file approach that avoids shared-append entirely — because two simultaneous CI jobs appending without coordination can produce torn NDJSON lines that the reader silently discards.
evidence: Plan Files: 'write_snapshot(dict) appends a timestamped record to a committed NDJSON file' — no mention of locking, serialization, or O_APPEND semantics
Plan Approach: 'Append-only and additive' — describes the property but not the mechanism for safe concurrent append
Codebase pattern: fsutil.atomic_write used universally (repo_snapshot.py, fsutil.py, compact.py, txn.py, hlc.py, etc.) — no existing append-write pattern to follow
POSIX O_APPEND is atomic only for writes ≤ PIPE_BUF (~4096 bytes) on local filesystems; network/shared filesystems (common in CI) do not guarantee this
findings.finding: The plan specifies `write_snapshot` as an append operation but does not address concurrent-write safety: two CI jobs appending to the same NDJSON file simultaneously can produce interleaved or truncated lines, yet the plan prescribes no locking or serialization strategy — the codebase's universal pattern for safe file mutation is `atomic_write` (temp+rename), which is not directly compatible with append semantics.
findings.location: Files section — `write_snapshot(dict)` description; Approach section
scenarios: Two parallel CI matrix jobs both invoke write_snapshot at the same second; both open the file in append mode and write concurrently; the resulting NDJSON line is interleaved/torn and the reader's malformed-line-skip logic discards both records silently
A CI runner on a network filesystem (NFS, EFS) where O_APPEND is not atomic produces partial writes that corrupt the NDJSON stream

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
